summary: Reviewed the changed server actions, RBAC helper, UI gates, and Supabase RLS/RPC migrations for access-control and data-integrity risks. The session-derived proposal creator, admin-client edit-context lookup, and reject RPC admin validation are sound. The main risks are in the database enforcement layer: direct authenticated updates can bypass the intended soft-delete rule, and the non-admin status trigger admits a needs_revisions-to-pending_approval transition that appears explicitly out of scope.
appears_sound: `src/actions/pre-event.ts:88` overwrites `p_payload.created_by` with `user.id`, so a client-supplied `created_by` field is ignored before the service-role RPC call.
`src/lib/events/edit-context.ts:21` uses the admin client to load the true event row for authorization, and `src/lib/events/edit-context.ts:30` logs DB errors before failing closed with `null`.
`src/lib/roles.ts:35` checks `deletedAt` before the office_worker creator/manager branches, and `src/lib/roles.ts:40` rejects non-office_worker creator edits after admin handling.
`supabase/migrations/20260420171000_reject_event_proposal_rpc.sql:16` validates `p_admin_id` as an active administrator inside the SECURITY DEFINER RPC.
`supabase/migrations/20260420170000_office_worker_event_scope.sql:120` keeps the event_artists creator branch to `draft` and `needs_revisions`, excluding `pending_approval`.

findings.description: OWASP A01/Broken Access Control: a direct authenticated Supabase client can update a soft-deleted event, or clear `deleted_at`, if they satisfy the creator or manager/status branch. Exploit scenario: an office_worker who created a draft later soft-deleted by an admin calls `events.update({ deleted_at: null })` and restores or mutates a row the app-level `canEditEvent` denies.
findings.evidence: The UPDATE policy at `supabase/migrations/20260420170000_office_worker_event_scope.sql:20` does not require `deleted_at IS NULL`, and the trigger only guards `venue_id`, `manager_responsible_id`, `created_by`, and `status` at `supabase/migrations/20260420170000_office_worker_event_scope.sql:61`.

findings.description: The non-admin trigger allowlist permits `needs_revisions` to move to `pending_approval`, which appears to violate the review pack requirement that `needs_revisions` is not in that allowlist. Exploit scenario: an office_worker creator directly updates a needs-revisions event into the proposal-only `pending_approval` state, corrupting the review workflow and admin triage state.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: This would be acceptable only if the spec intentionally changed needs-revisions resubmission to use `pending_approval` rather than the normal review status.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check the v3.2 spec's non-admin status transition list and whether needs-revisions resubmission should target `submitted` or `pending_approval`.

findings.description: `deleteEventAction` now authorizes destructive deletion with the same `canEditEvent` predicate used for normal edits, which grants office_worker managers on approved/cancelled events delete power. Exploit scenario: a venue manager assigned as `manager_responsible_id` can submit `deleteEventAction` for an approved live event and soft-delete it, even though the previous delete rule was admin-or-own-draft/needs-revisions only.
findings.evidence: `src/actions/events.ts:1888` rejects deletion only when `canEditEvent(...)` fails, while `src/lib/roles.ts:51` returns true for an office_worker manager on `approved` or `cancelled` events.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The implementation comments suggest delete may have been intentionally classified as an edit/update call-site in this feature.
findings.what_would_confirm: Product/security owner should confirm whether office_worker managers may soft-delete approved/cancelled events, or whether delete needs a narrower `canDeleteEvent` rule.

findings.description: Proposal idempotency is not effective for the normal form path because the form does not send a stable idempotency key and the action generates a new UUID per request. Exploit scenario: an authenticated user or replaying client submits the same proposal multiple times without `idempotencyKey`, creating duplicate pending events and audit rows instead of returning the first result.
findings.evidence: `src/actions/pre-event.ts:82` falls back to `randomUUID()` when `idempotencyKey` is absent, and `src/components/events/propose-event-form.tsx:40` renders the proposal form fields without any hidden `idempotencyKey` input.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The submit button may reduce accidental double-clicks in the browser, but it does not protect direct posts, retries, or clients with JavaScript disabled.

findings.description: Attachment upload UI is still gated by same-venue scope rather than the new event edit predicate, so it may expose file-upload mutation controls to office_workers who cannot edit the event. Exploit scenario: a non-manager office_worker at the same venue uploads or alters attachments on an event that `canEditEvent` would deny, if the attachment server action has the same stale venue-scoped authorization.
findings.evidence: `src/app/events/[eventId]/page.tsx:139` computes `canUploadAttachments` as `administrator || isVenueScoped`, while nearby edit/delete/booking controls use `canEditEventFromRow`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The attachment upload server action may independently enforce the stricter event edit rules, but that code is outside the review pack.
findings.what_would_confirm: Review the attachment upload/delete server actions for a fresh `getCurrentUser` check plus `canEditEvent` or equivalent row-scoped authorization.

empty_categories: No hardcoded secrets or API keys were introduced in the changed files included in the pack.
No unauthenticated state-changing action was introduced; changed actions call `getCurrentUser` before mutation paths.
No `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` or raw HTML rendering path was introduced in the changed UI files.
No raw SQL string interpolation was introduced in the application TypeScript shown in the pack.
